svg.text: Neutron Stars: Matter at the
Edge of Collapse
How the violent death of a massive star can leave behind an object
the size of a city and as dense as the nucleus of an atom.
The Death That Leaves a Heart
Behind
A star many times heavier than the Sun does not end its life gently. For millions of
years it fuses hydrogen into helium, helium into carbon, and onward through
ever-heavier elements, wrapping itself in concentric shells like the layers of an
onion. But this chain of fusion halts abruptly at iron. Forging elements heavier
than iron consumes energy instead of releasing it, so the moment the core becomes
iron, the furnace that has held the star against its own gravity simply goes out.
What follows is among the most violent events in the cosmos. Stripped of pressure
support, the iron core — already heavier than the Sun yet smaller than the Earth —
collapses in a fraction of a second. Protons and electrons are crushed together into
neutrons, unleashing a torrent of ghostly particles called neutrinos. The infalling
envelope slams into the suddenly rigid neutron core, rebounds, and the star
detonates as a core-collapse supernova that can briefly outshine its entire host
galaxy.
The whole sequence unfolds with breathtaking speed:
1. A star born with more than roughly eight times the Sun's mass burns its
way up to an inert iron core.
2. Iron cannot yield energy through fusion, so pressure support at the centre
collapses.
3. In well under a second the core implodes, fusing electrons and protons
into neutrons.
4. Neutron degeneracy pressure, reinforced by the strong nuclear force,
abruptly arrests the fall.
5. The rebound and a flood of neutrinos blow the outer layers off in a
supernova.
6. The exposed core survives as a neutron star — or, if it is heavy enough,
as a black hole.
What remains at the centre is a neutron star: a sphere of almost pure neutrons
holding one to two times the mass of the Sun inside a ball little more than twenty
